# durable-sync

> Offline-first sync for Cloudflare Durable Objects. An append-only op log on the server, a durable outbox on the client. Zero dependencies, no Postgres, no long-running container, no WebSocket. TypeScript, MIT.

durable-sync is a primitive, not a database. A Durable Object is already a single-threaded ordering point per user ‚Äî the thing other sync engines need Postgres for ‚Äî so the remaining work is an outbox, a cursor, and honesty about what has actually been acknowledged.

Install: `npm i durable-sync`. Two entry points: `durable-sync/server` and `durable-sync/client`.

## Core model

- Ops are opaque immutable facts: `{ opId, kind, payload }`. The log never interprets a payload.
- The server assigns `seq`, monotonic within an `epoch`. Pushes are idempotent by `opId`.
- The client holds a durable outbox (must be IndexedDB ‚Äî until a push is acknowledged the outbox may hold the only copy of a write) and a per-device cursor `{ seq, epoch }`.
- `apply(op)` is supplied by the caller and MUST be idempotent; an op can arrive more than once.
- A pull carrying a new `epoch` makes the client replay from 0. This is how a reset log is survivable.

## Server API (`durable-sync/server`)

- `SyncJournal` ‚Äî extend and export from your Worker. Routes, reachable only through a binding: `POST /push` ‚Üí `{ seq, accepted }`; `GET /pull?since=N` ‚Üí `{ ops, seq, epoch }`; `GET /ops?kind=K` ‚Üí `{ ops }`; `DELETE /reset` ‚Üí `{ cleared, epoch }`.
- `SqlOpStore`, `MemoryOpStore` ‚Äî implementations of `OpStore`.
- `handlePush`, `handlePull`, `handleReset`, `handleOpsByKind` ‚Äî the pure logic, testable without a Workers runtime.
- Address one instance per user: `ns.get(ns.idFromName(userKey))`.

## Client API (`durable-sync/client`)

- `createSync(options)` ‚Üí `{ now, enqueue, start, subscribe, getState, getServerState }`.
- Options: `endpoint`, `outbox`, `cursor`, `apply`, `canPull`, `canWrite`, `stateKey`, `minIntervalMs`, `pollMs`, `headers`, `timeoutMs`.
- `localStorageCursor(key)`, `memoryCursor()`, `memoryOutbox()` ‚Äî adapters.
- `start()` wires `visibilitychange` / `pageshow` / `focus` / `online` plus a poll, single-flighted and throttled; returns a stop function. Call it from an effect, not module scope.
- `getState` / `subscribe` / `getServerState` are shaped for React's `useSyncExternalStore`. Pass `getServerState` as the third argument ‚Äî an inline `() => ({})` makes React throw.
- The transport is deliberately not exported: reaching past `createSync` bypasses `canWrite()`.

## The four failure modes it exists to prevent

- `res.ok` is not evidence. An auth proxy with an expired session redirects to a same-origin login page; fetch follows it and reports 200. The reply's shape is validated before the outbox is drained.
- A cursor only means something against the log that issued it. Every pull carries an `epoch`; a new one triggers replay from 0, otherwise a reset log leaves clients silently stalled forever.
- Pulling is not always safe. `canPull()` defers a pull that could disturb work in progress. Pushing is always safe.
- The gate must be the only door. `canWrite()` is checked first and `force` never skips it.

## What it does not do

No conflict resolution (last writer wins per record). No pagination (a pull returns everything after the cursor). No live push (converges on next foreground). No auth. One `createSync` instance per identity. Nothing runs while the app is closed ‚Äî Safari has no Background Sync.

If you need those, use a real sync engine ‚Äî Zero, Electric, or PowerSync ‚Äî and the Postgres that comes with it. If you need merge, use a CRDT: Yjs or Automerge.

## Links

- Docs: https://isaacrowntree.github.io/durable-sync/
- Comparison vs Zero, Electric, PowerSync, partysync: https://isaacrowntree.github.io/durable-sync/vs.html
- Source: https://github.com/isaacrowntree/durable-sync
- npm: https://www.npmjs.com/package/durable-sync
